Take-Home Assignment

Extend an open-source video editor, then test it with an agent

Take a real, open-source desktop video editor and add a set of modern, CapCut-style editing features to it. Then expose the editor to an AI agent through an MCP server, and have an agent drive the app end-to-end to exercise each feature you built — capturing both a step-by-step trajectory and a trimmed screen recording of the essential steps. We are testing two things at once: your ability to ship real features inside an unfamiliar C++/Qt codebase, and your ability to make that app agent-operable and prove it works.

The app
Shotcut — open-source cross-platform video editor (Qt / C++ / MLT)
Design reference
CapCut — match its behavior & UX for each feature (see the reference screenshots)
Time
4–8 focused hours. Timebox it — a few features done well beats all six half-built.
You submit
A public fork of Shotcut with your features, the MCP integration, agent trajectories + recordings, and an HTML writeup

1.Context

We build and evaluate AI agents that operate real desktop applications. A great way to stress that is to take a mature open-source app, extend it with genuinely useful features, and then see whether an agent can drive those features on its own. This assignment is that loop end-to-end: build features in a real codebase, expose the app to an agent via MCP, and demonstrate the agent using what you built — with evidence, not claims.

2.The app & the reference

You will work on Shotcut, a real, actively-maintained cross-platform video editor (Qt/QML front-end, C++ core, MLT engine). Clone it, build it, and edit a clip in it before you write any code.

  • Repo: github.com/mltframework/shotcut — fork it (public) and build on current master.
  • CapCut is your design reference. For each feature, match CapCut's behavior and interaction model as closely as the Shotcut UI reasonably allows. The reference screenshots (the attached PDF) show exactly where each feature lives in CapCut and what it should do.
Sample footage. You'll need a clip to edit and to test the features on. Grab any YouTube video you like (e.g. a short trailer, vlog, or music video — the reference screenshots use a Game of Thrones recap) and download it with a tool like yt-dlp, then import it into Shotcut. Anything with clear cuts, motion, and speech works well for showing off transitions, speed ramps, captions, and denoise.

3.Features to build

Implement the CapCut-style features below inside Shotcut. Each row lists the feature, where it lives in CapCut, and what "done" looks like. Prioritize and timebox — implement as many as you can do well, and be explicit about what you completed vs. skipped.

#FeatureWhat it should do (CapCut reference)
1 Transitions
转场 · dissolve / wipe
 Drag-to-apply transitions between two clips — at minimum dissolve and a wipe family (radial, linear, clock, etc.), with adjustable duration.
2 Speed curve (speed ramp)
变速曲线 · speed-curve
 Non-linear time remapping via an editable velocity curve, with presets (e.g. Montage, Hero, Bullet, Jump Cut, Flash in/out) plus a custom, keyframe-editable curve.
3 Keyframes
关键帧 · key frame
 Add/edit keyframes on clip properties (position, scale, rotation, opacity…) — any animatable property gets a keyframe toggle and interpolates over time.
4 Clip animations
动画 · animation
 Preset In / Out / Combo animations applied to a clip (fade, zoom, slide, blur, etc.) with adjustable duration.
5 Captions & text templates
动画文字/字幕模板 · auto captions & text templates
 Auto-generate captions from the clip's audio and/or apply animated text templates to titles. (Speech-to-text may use an existing library/model — call out what you used.)
6 Audio: denoise & separation
降噪/分离音轨 · reduce noise & separate audio
 Reduce noise on an audio track, and separate a track into stems (e.g. vocals vs. instruments). (Separation may lean on an existing model — state which.)
Match the outcome, not the pixels. You don't need to reproduce CapCut's exact panel layout — you need to reproduce the capability and the editing result. If a user could do it in CapCut, they should be able to do the equivalent in your Shotcut, and the exported video should reflect it.

3b.Reference screenshots (CapCut)

These are the exact CapCut panels for each feature above — use them as the behavioral and UX reference for your Shotcut implementation. Click to open full size.

4.Make the app agent-operable (MCP)

Expose the editor to an AI agent through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server so an agent can drive it programmatically — open a project, add clips, and invoke the features you built (apply a transition, set a speed curve, add keyframes, run denoise, etc.) and read back enough state to know it worked.

  • Design a small, clear tool surface — one tool per meaningful editor action, with typed args and useful return values.
  • Cover the features you shipped in §3: if you built transitions and keyframes, the agent must be able to apply and inspect them via MCP.
  • Document how to launch the MCP server against your build, and list the tools it exposes.

5.Have an agent test the features

Point a coding/computer-use agent at your MCP-enabled build and have it exercise each feature you shipped, autonomously. Capture what it did and prove the feature works from the agent's side.

Trajectory

Record what the agent did

For each feature, capture the agent's trajectory: the sequence of MCP tool calls (and reasoning, if available), inputs, and results — enough that a reader can follow exactly how the agent produced the edit and confirm the outcome (e.g. the exported clip shows the transition / speed ramp / caption).

Recording

Record a trimmed video of the essential steps

Produce a screen recording of the agent operating the app. Agents are slow step-to-step — so streamline it to the essential steps: trim dead time and cut to the moments that matter (feature invoked → visible result), so the clip is concentrated and easy to watch. One tight recording per feature, or one combined highlight reel, is fine.

6.Deliverables

Submit a public GitHub fork of Shotcut, forked from current master. In it:

  • Your feature work — the implemented features from §3, buildable, with a note on what's complete vs. partial.
  • The MCP integration — the server/tools, plus instructions to run it against your build.
  • Agent trajectories — one per shipped feature, showing the tool calls and the confirmed result.
  • Screen recordings — trimmed to the essential steps (see §5).
  • A viewable HTML writeup (~1 page, opens in a browser, no build step) with visuals: what you built, how you mapped CapCut behavior into Shotcut, how the MCP layer works, and the agent results. Show, don't just tell.

7.How we evaluate you

DimensionWhat we look for
Feature qualityThe features actually work in a real build and produce the correct edit on export — faithful to the CapCut reference. The core axis.
Codebase navigationYou found the right seams in a large unfamiliar C++/Qt/MLT codebase and made clean, idiomatic changes.
MCP designA clear, well-typed tool surface that genuinely lets an agent drive the app and read back state.
Agent evidenceTrajectories + trimmed recordings that convincingly show an agent using each feature — concise and honest.
CraftClean diff, clear writeup, reproducible build/run, honest scoping of what's done.

A few features that truly work — agent-drivable and demonstrated — beat six half-built ones. We value a correct, honest end-to-end slice over breadth.

App: Shotcut (mltframework) · Reference: CapCut
Qt / C++ / MLTCapCut-style features MCP integrationagent trajectory screen recording
